agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show real-time courier location to customers during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly conflicting, non-negotiable requirements about whether courier GPS data should be exposed to customers.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Shipment Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A driver or delivery person who physically transports a parcel from origin to the customer's delivery address."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to a customer's delivery address."},{"term":"Location visibility","definition":"The degree to which a courier's position is exposed to external parties such as customers."},{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — a derived value communicated to the customer without exposing raw GPS coordinates."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery must have exactly one assigned courier whose location is tracked internally by the operations platform.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without internal location tracking, dispatch, routing, and SLA management are impossible. This is a data integrity requirement.","testable_assertion":"Given an active delivery, the system always has a courier assignment with a valid, recent GPS fix stored internally."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Customer-facing location data, if exposed, must only be available during the active delivery window (courier en route to customer).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Exposing courier location outside the delivery window would constitute tracking a private individual during non-work activity — a privacy and legal violation.","testable_assertion":"Given a delivery that is not in 'en route' status, no courier location data is retrievable via any customer-facing API or UI."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The system must provide customers with delivery progress information sufficient to plan for receipt of their parcel.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The form of progress information (exact GPS vs. ETA vs. status milestones) is a business policy decision, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an active delivery, the customer can retrieve at least one form of progress indicator (e.g., status update, ETA, or proximity zone)."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Courier privacy preferences regarding location sharing must be captured and respected by the platform.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a workforce policy that could be adjusted, but its current framing by Operations is that ignoring it causes driver attrition — a serious business risk, not a system-corrupt scenario.","testable_assertion":"Given a courier who has opted out of direct location sharing, no exact GPS coordinates are exposed to customers for that courier's deliveries."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery is in 'en route' status","when":"The customer views delivery tracking","then":"The customer sees at least an ETA or delivery status update (the exact form of location disclosure is BLOCKED pending stakeholder resolution)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A delivery is not yet dispatched or has been completed","when":"The customer attempts to view courier location","then":"No courier location or tracking data is available; the system returns the delivery's current status only"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The system receives a GPS fix from a courier's device","when":"The fix is stored internally","then":"The raw GPS coordinates are persisted in the operations-internal tracking store and are never directly forwarded to customer-facing APIs without passing through the visibility policy layer"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A courier has a privacy preference set to 'no direct location sharing'","when":"A customer requests tracking for that courier's delivery","then":"The system returns a derived indicator (ETA, proximity zone, or status milestone) instead of raw coordinates"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier's GPS signal is lost mid-delivery","expected_behavior":"Customer sees last known status/ETA with a 'last updated' timestamp; system does not fabricate location data."},{"scenario":"Customer contacts support asking 'where exactly is my driver?'","expected_behavior":"Support agents see internal GPS for operational purposes but communicate ETA or zone-level info to the customer, consistent with the customer-facing policy."},{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for an extended period (e.g., traffic, break)","expected_behavior":"System continues to update ETA based on real-time conditions; does not expose that the courier is stationary at specific coordinates."},{"scenario":"Multiple deliveries on same route — courier serves another customer first","expected_behavior":"Each customer sees only their own delivery's ETA/status, not the courier's detour to another address."},{"scenario":"Regulatory request (law enforcement) for courier location history","expected_behavior":"Internal GPS logs are retained per data-retention policy and can be produced under lawful request; customer-facing visibility policy is irrelevant to this path."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Unresolved, this conflict blocks a revenue-impacting feature. Choosing wrong could either increase customer churn (no transparency) or increase driver churn (surveillance). Driver replacement costs in courier logistics typically run $3K–$5K per driver; customer acquisition cost may be comparable. Both sides carry real P&L risk.","operational":"Engineering cannot build the feature without a clear visibility policy. Any interim implementation risks rework. Driver retention directly affects delivery capacity and SLAs.","user":"Customers want confidence their parcel is arriving. Couriers want dignity and privacy. Both are legitimate user needs. A middle-ground (ETA, proximity zones) likely satisfies both, but this has not been validated with either group.","risk":"HIGH — shipping either extreme (full GPS or zero visibility) without resolution risks regulatory exposure (GDPR/privacy for GPS sharing without worker consent) or competitive disadvantage (no tracking at all). Additionally, building to one team's spec and then reversing creates technical debt and erodes trust.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — they want delivery visibility to plan their day and feel confident in the service."},{"group":"Couriers / Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — exact GPS sharing makes them feel surveilled, contributing to attrition in a tight labor market."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Their KPIs (NPS, CSAT) depend on transparency features; blocking this undermines their goals."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Their KPIs (driver retention, delivery capacity) are harmed by surveillance perception; they need driver trust."},{"group":"Product / Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict — it is a strategic decision about brand positioning (transparency vs. driver welfare)."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Real-time worker GPS sharing to third parties has privacy regulation implications (GDPR Art. 6/9, state biometric/location laws) that neither team has addressed."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The specification contains a direct, explicit contradiction between two non-negotiable stakeholder requirements. Both positions are clearly stated and mutually exclusive as written. No arbiter has weighed in. Additionally, legal/regulatory implications of GPS sharing with customers have not been addressed by either party, adding further uncertainty. Confidence is low because no correct implementation can be defined until this conflict is resolved at the executive level.","high_confidence_areas":["Internal courier GPS tracking is required for operations regardless of the customer-facing decision","Customer-facing visibility must be limited to the active delivery window","A policy abstraction layer between raw GPS and customer-facing APIs is architecturally necessary no matter the outcome","Both teams' concerns are legitimate and carry real business risk"],"low_confidence_areas":["Legal/regulatory position on sharing worker GPS with customers has not been stated by anyone in the scenario","Whether a middle-ground (ETA/proximity zones) would satisfy Customer Success's 'transparency' requirement","Driver consent model and employment law implications","Data retention and GDPR compliance requirements for location data"],"assumptions":["Both teams have authority to raise requirements but neither has unilateral authority to override the other","The platform already collects courier GPS internally for dispatch/routing","No legal review of customer-facing GPS sharing has been completed"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS coordinates shown to customers at all times during delivery. Operations requires courier location never be shown to customers. These are explicitly mutually exclusive as stated — one demands full exposure, the other demands zero exposure. Both claim non-negotiability. This cannot be resolved at the engineering or domain level; it requires executive arbitration.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Success","Operations","Couriers","Customers","Executive Leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["(A) Side with Customer Success: show exact GPS. Risk: driver attrition accelerates, potential privacy regulation violations.","(B) Side with Operations: show no location data. Risk: competitive disadvantage, customer dissatisfaction, CS team goals unmet.","(C) Propose a compromise: show derived data (ETA, proximity zone like ' 3 stops away', status milestones) instead of raw GPS. Satisfies transparency intent without surveillance. Requires both teams to accept a middle ground.","(D) Make it courier-opt-in: couriers choose whether to share location. Respects driver autonomy but creates inconsistent customer experience."],"recommendation":"Option C — derived visibility (ETA + proximity zones + status milestones). This is the industry-standard approach used by UPS, FedEx, Amazon, and DoorDash. It gives customers the planning ability they actually need without exposing raw GPS. However, this recommendation MUST be validated with both teams and blessed by executive leadership before any implementation begins. Legal review of even derived location data is also recommended."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"Neither team has acknowledged or addressed the legal and privacy implications of sharing real-time worker location with customers. GDPR, CCPA, and employment law may constrain the solution space independently of either team's preference. This is a secondary gap exposed by the primary contradiction.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Executive Leadership"],"options":["(A) Proceed without legal review — high regulatory risk.","(B) Block feature until legal review is complete — delays delivery but prevents compliance exposure."],"recommendation":"Option B — legal review must be completed before any customer-facing location feature ships. This is non-negotiable from a compliance standpoint regardless of which team 'wins' the policy debate."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement exact GPS sharing to customers as specified by Customer Success","business_rule_broken":"Directly contradicts Operations' requirement and raises unaddressed privacy/legal risks (BR-4, workforce dignity and consent)"},{"violation":"Cannot implement zero location visibility as specified by Operations","business_rule_broken":"Directly contradicts Customer Success's requirement and ignores legitimate customer need for delivery progress (BR-3, delivery progress information)"},{"violation":"No implementation should proceed until the contradiction is resolved by executive leadership and legal review is completed","business_rule_broken":"Building to either unilateral spec guarantees rework, stakeholder conflict, and potential regulatory exposure"}]}
